title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a woman called Samira Khan, who designs clothes from recycled materials.
audio_script: Interviewer: Samira Khan designs clothes from recycled materials. Samira, were you interested in fashion when you were young? || Guest: I liked clothes, but I didn't dream of becoming a designer. My mother repaired everything instead of throwing things away, and I used to think that was embarrassing. Later, at college, [20]I made a jacket from an old tent for a project, and everyone wanted to know where I had bought it[/20]. That changed my attitude. || Interviewer: What was hard when you started your business? || Guest: Finding materials wasn't hard because people had plenty to give me. Selling online was simple too. [21]The difficult part was making each item strong enough to wear often, not just interesting to look at[/21]. I had to test everything. || Interviewer: How do customers react to your clothes? || Guest: Some are already interested in the environment, but others just like the designs. [22]I feel happiest when someone chooses a coat because it looks good and only later discovers it is recycled[/22]. Then the idea seems normal. || Interviewer: Do you follow fashion trends? || Guest: I look at them, but I don't copy them closely. Trends change quickly, and that creates waste. [23]I prefer making simple shapes that people can keep wearing for several years[/23]. That's better for the planet. || Interviewer: You also run workshops. What happens there? || Guest: People bring old clothes and we change them. At first they worry about making mistakes. [24]By the end, they're usually proud because they've created something useful with their own hands[/24]. That's lovely to see. || Interviewer: What are your plans for the future? || Guest: Shops have asked to sell my clothes, which is exciting, but I want to grow carefully. [25]My next goal is to make video lessons so people in other places can learn the same skills[/25]. || Interviewer: Before we finish, Samira, do you think people are becoming more careful about what they buy? || Guest: Slowly, yes. Many people now ask where materials come from, which almost nobody did when I started. But price is still important, and I understand that. Recycled clothing should not feel like a luxury that only a few people can afford. That is why I try to keep my designs simple and use processes that don't waste time or material. || Interviewer: What do you do with pieces that don't work? || Guest: I keep them. A failed sleeve might become a pocket, or a small piece of cloth might become part of a bag. Having boxes of odd pieces can look messy, but they are useful. It also reminds me that mistakes don't always have to be thrown away. Sometimes they just need another purpose. || Interviewer: Has your family changed the way they shop because of your work? || Guest: Definitely. My brother used to buy new clothes constantly, and now he asks me whether something can be repaired. He still likes fashion, but he thinks more carefully. I see that as a small victory. || Interviewer: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas? || Guest: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Samira Khan designs clothes from recycled materials. Samira, were you interested in fashion when you were young?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I liked clothes, but I didn't dream of becoming a designer. My mother repaired everything instead of throwing things away, and I used to think that was embarrassing. Later, at college, I made a jacket from an old tent for a project, and everyone wanted to know where I had bought it. That changed my attitude.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What was hard when you started your business?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Finding materials wasn't hard because people had plenty to give me. Selling online was simple too. The difficult part was making each item strong enough to wear often, not just interesting to look at. I had to test everything.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: How do customers react to your clothes?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Some are already interested in the environment, but others just like the designs. I feel happiest when someone chooses a coat because it looks good and only later discovers it is recycled. Then the idea seems normal.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you follow fashion trends?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I look at them, but I don't copy them closely. Trends change quickly, and that creates waste. I prefer making simple shapes that people can keep wearing for several years. That's better for the planet.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: You also run workshops. What happens there?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: People bring old clothes and we change them. At first they worry about making mistakes. By the end, they're usually proud because they've created something useful with their own hands. That's lovely to see.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What are your plans for the future?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Shops have asked to sell my clothes, which is exciting, but I want to grow carefully. My next goal is to make video lessons so people in other places can learn the same skills.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Before we finish, Samira, do you think people are becoming more careful about what they buy?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Slowly, yes. Many people now ask where materials come from, which almost nobody did when I started. But price is still important, and I understand that. Recycled clothing should not feel like a luxury that only a few people can afford. That is why I try to keep my designs simple and use processes that don't waste time or material.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do you do with pieces that don't work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I keep them. A failed sleeve might become a pocket, or a small piece of cloth might become part of a bag. Having boxes of odd pieces can look messy, but they are useful. It also reminds me that mistakes don't always have to be thrown away. Sometimes they just need another purpose.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has your family changed the way they shop because of your work?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Definitely. My brother used to buy new clothes constantly, and now he asks me whether something can be repaired. He still likes fashion, but he thinks more carefully. I see that as a small victory.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Finally, what do you do when you need new ideas?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I try to step away from the main work for a short time. Going for a walk, visiting another place or talking to someone outside the job usually helps. If I sit at a desk worrying, nothing improves. I also keep a small notebook, because useful ideas often arrive at inconvenient moments, like on a bus or while making dinner.

questions.question: What changed Samira's attitude to recycled fashion?

options.B: learning repair skills from her mother
options.C: buying a jacket made from old material

questions.explanation: Samira says people admired a jacket she made from an old tent for a college project.

questions.question: What did Samira find difficult at the start of her business?
questions.explanation: She says the difficult part was making items strong enough to wear often.

questions.question: When does Samira feel happiest about a customer?

options.A: when they already care about the environment
options.B: when they ask how something was made
options.C: when they like the design before knowing it is recycled

questions.explanation: Samira says she is happiest when someone chooses a coat because it looks good and later discovers it is recycled.

questions.question: Why does Samira avoid following trends closely?

options.A: They cause people to waste clothes.
options.B: They are difficult to copy well.
options.C: They do not suit her customers.

questions.explanation: She says trends change quickly and create waste, so she prefers simple shapes that last.

questions.question: How do people usually feel at the end of Samira's workshops?

options.A: surprised by how easy sewing is
options.B: pleased with what they have made

questions.explanation: Samira says people are usually proud because they have created something useful themselves.

questions.question: What does Samira want to do next?
questions.explanation: She says her next goal is to make video lessons so people elsewhere can learn the same skills.
